programs.name: AHCCCS (Arizona Long Term Care System - ALTCS)
programs.tagline: Arizona's Medicaid program covering nursing home costs, home care, and assisted living for qualifying adults 65+ or disabled.

localEntities.name: ALTCS Map available via official site

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management but can't afford long-term care, ALTCS may cover the full cost. Because ALTCS is Arizona's Medicaid long-term care program, it pays for nursing homes, assisted living, memory care, and home care services — allowing your parent to receive care in their preferred setting rather than being forced into a facility.

To qualify, your parent must be 65+ (or disabled at any age), have assets under $2,000 ($4,000 for couples), and pass a clinical assessment proving they need nursing facility level care. ALTCS also allows family members to be paid as caregivers through the Difficulty of Care program, turning unpaid family caregiving into compensated work.

Unlike traditional Medicaid, ALTCS coordinates all long-term care under one program, so your parent won't juggle multiple providers or authorization systems. One team manages everything from medical care to daily assistance.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need Nursing Facility Level of Care (assessed through Pre-Admission Screening evaluating ability to transfer, eat, toilet, bathe, dress, and cognitive function)
otherRequirements: Arizona resident
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Social Security Number

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Health-e-Arizona Plus or call (888) 621-6880 — processing time varies month-to-month.

steps.title: Start your application
steps.description: Apply online at Health-e-Arizona Plus ALTCS portal, call (888) 621-6880, or complete form DE-828

steps.title: Submit financial documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of income, assets, citizenship, residency, and Social Security Number

steps.title: Schedule Pre-Admission Screening
steps.description: Complete clinical assessment (PAS) to determine if you meet Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements

steps.title: Await eligibility determination
steps.description: AHCCCS reviews both financial and medical eligibility — decisions made monthly

steps.title: Choose care setting
steps.description: If approved, select from home care, AHCCCS-certified nursing facility, or assisted living facility

applicationGuide.processingTime: Eligibility determined month-by-month; renewal every 12 months
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent's income exceeds limits, ask about Income Only Trust options before assuming they don't qualify.

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for applicant and spouse
Proof of age (birth certificate or passport)
Proof of Arizona residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Proof of U.S. citizenship or qualified immigrant status
Most recent Social Security award letter
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Property deeds and tax statements
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with cash values
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Medical records documenting need for daily assistance
Current medication list and prescribing physicians
Documentation of current care arrangements or services

contacts.label: ALTCS Application Line
contacts.description: Apply for ALTCS or check application status

contacts.label: AHCCCS Customer Service
contacts.description: General AHCCCS questions and member services

applicationNotes: If only one spouse applies, both spouses' combined assets are counted — but community spouse protections may allow keeping $32,532-$162,660
Income earned by family members providing Difficulty of Care to an ALTCS member is excluded from income calculations
Transfer penalties vary by region: $8,666.72/month in Maricopa, Pima, Pinal Counties vs $8,132.22/month elsewhere
Medical diagnosis alone doesn't qualify — you must pass functional assessment proving need for nursing facility level care

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than $2,000?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt as long as equity doesn't exceed $752,000 and your parent intends to return or certain family members live there. The $2,000 asset limit applies to countable assets like savings accounts and additional properties.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high but they still can't afford care?
faqs.answer: They may qualify through an Income Only Trust, which allows people over income limits to become eligible. Don't assume disqualification based on income alone — call (888) 621-6880 to discuss options.

faqs.question: Can I get paid to care for my parent through ALTCS?
faqs.answer: Yes, ALTCS allows family members to be paid as caregivers through Medicaid's Difficulty of Care program. Income earned this way is also excluded from eligibility calculations if other family members apply for ALTCS.

faqs.question: What's the difference between needing help and qualifying medically?
faqs.answer: ALTCS requires Nursing Facility Level of Care, assessed through Pre-Admission Screening. This evaluates ability to transfer, eat, toilet, bathe, dress, and cognitive function. Simply having a diagnosis or needing some help doesn't automatically qualify.

faqs.question: Can my parent choose to stay home instead of going to a nursing facility?
faqs.answer: Yes, ALTCS specifically provides in-home services to help people avoid nursing home placement. Your parent can receive care in their own home, an assisted living facility, or a nursing facility.

faqs.question: What happens to my parent's spouse if only one needs ALTCS?
faqs.answer: Community spouse protections allow the non-applying spouse to keep between $32,532 and $162,660 in assets, determined case-by-case. The community spouse can also remain in the family home regardless of equity value.

faqs.question: How long does the assessment process take?
faqs.answer: The Pre-Admission Screening (PAS) is scheduled after your application is submitted. AHCCCS makes eligibility determinations monthly, so timing depends on when you apply in the month and how quickly you complete all requirements.

faqs.question: What if we gave money to family or sold assets recently?
faqs.answer: ALTCS has transfer penalties for assets given away or sold below market value. Penalty periods vary by region: $8,666.72 monthly penalty rate in Maricopa/Pima/Pinal counties, $8,132.22 elsewhere. Consult an elder law attorney before making transfers.

programs.name: ALTCS Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waiver
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication but wants to stay home instead of a nursing facility, Arizona may pay for up to 40 hours per week of in-home care.

localEntities.name: Approved plan providers (e.g., via altcs.org list; contact AHCCCS for local)

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, cooking, or managing medications but wants to stay home instead of moving to a nursing facility, Arizona's ALTCS HCBS Waiver may cover up to 40 hours per week of in-home personal care. This program is designed for people 65+ who qualify for nursing home-level care but choose to receive services at home or in the community.

The program covers personal care attendants who can help with activities of daily living like bathing, dressing, grooming, toileting, transferring, and mobility assistance. It also includes help with cooking, medication management, basic life skills, and transportation to medical appointments. Family members — including spouses and adult children — can be paid caregivers if hired through an approved provider.

Because ALTCS uses strict income limits ($2,982/month for individuals in 2026) and asset limits ($2,000 total), plus requires a clinical assessment proving your parent needs nursing facility-level care, most applicants need help navigating the qualification process. The program operates statewide through managed care providers, but availability and wait times vary by region.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care through Pre-Admission Screening — a clinical assessment that evaluates whether your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or transferring, plus cognitive deficits from conditions like dementia.
otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or qualified legal resident
Valid Social Security number
Must live in approved setting (own home, licensed medical facility, or waiver-approved residence)
Must attempt to secure other entitled government benefits

applicationGuide.summary: Call AHCCCS at 1-855-432-7587 to start your application, which involves both financial screening and a medical assessment that can take several weeks to complete.

steps.title: Call AHCCCS to initiate application
steps.description: Call 1-855-432-7587 for general AHCCCS services and ask to start an ALTCS application, or contact your local ALTCS office directly.

steps.title: Complete form DE-828
steps.description: Fill out the Filing an Application for the Arizona Long Term Care System (ALTCS) form, available online at azahcccs.gov or from your local office.

steps.title: Submit financial documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of income, assets, medical history, and physician information to establish financial eligibility.

steps.title: Schedule Pre-Admission Screening (PAS)
steps.description: A caseworker will schedule a medical assessment interview to determine if your parent meets Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

steps.title: Choose managed care provider
steps.description: If approved, select from available approved plan providers in your area who will coordinate and deliver home care services.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time varies — involves financial evaluation followed by medical screening and interview by assigned caseworker
applicationGuide.tip: Gather all medical records showing your parent's need for daily assistance before applying — the clinical assessment is just as important as meeting income limits.

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for applicant and spouse
Proof of age (birth certificate or passport)
Proof of Arizona residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities)
Property documents (deeds, tax statements)
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with face values
Pre-paid burial contracts or funeral arrangements
Complete medical history and current physician information
Medical records documenting need for daily assistance with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or mobility
Prescription medication lists and management needs
Evidence of attempts to secure other government benefits

contacts.label: AHCCCS General Line
contacts.description: Start ALTCS application and general AHCCCS questions

contacts.label: Arizona 2-1-1
contacts.description: 24/7 helpline for all social services and local ALTCS office referrals

applicationNotes: Both spouses must apply together if married — income and assets are combined for eligibility determination
The 60-month Look-Back Rule applies — any asset transfers below fair market value in the past 5 years create penalty periods
Family caregivers can be paid, but must be hired by the approved provider, not directly by you
Dementia diagnosis alone doesn't guarantee approval — you must prove need for daily physical assistance through the clinical assessment
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than $2,000?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit as long as your parent lives there or intends to return, and the home equity doesn't exceed $752,000 in 2026. If your parent moves permanently to a care facility, the home may become countable unless a spouse or disabled child lives there.

faqs.question: Can I be paid to take care of my parent through this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members including spouses and adult children can be paid caregivers, but you must be hired by the approved managed care provider — not paid directly by your parent or the state. The provider handles payroll, taxes, and ensures you meet any training requirements.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is $3,000/month — just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Your parent won't qualify with income over $2,982/month unless they can reduce countable income. However, income from providing care to another ALTCS member in the home may be excluded under Difficulty of Care rules. Consult with AHCCCS to review all possible income exclusions.

faqs.question: How long does the medical assessment really take?
faqs.answer: The Pre-Admission Screening involves an in-person interview with a caseworker who evaluates your parent's ability to perform activities of daily living and cognitive function. The assessment itself takes a few hours, but scheduling and processing the results can add weeks to your application timeline.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply while they're in the hospital?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can start the application process while your parent is hospitalized. Hospital discharge planners often help initiate ALTCS applications for patients who need ongoing care but want to avoid nursing facility placement. Starting early gives you more options for discharge planning.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's condition gets worse after enrollment?
faqs.answer: The managed care provider will reassess your parent's needs and can increase service hours up to the program maximum (40 hours/week for family caregivers, 50 hours/week for non-family). More intensive needs may require transitioning to nursing facility care or other ALTCS services.

faqs.question: Are there waiting lists, and do they vary by region?
faqs.answer: The program operates through managed care providers statewide, and availability can vary by region and provider capacity. Some areas may have immediate openings while others experience delays. Ask about current availability when choosing your managed care provider during the application process.

faqs.question: Can my parent have both Medicare and ALTCS services?
faqs.answer: Yes, ALTCS coordinates with Medicare and other insurance. Your parent keeps their Medicare coverage for medical services, while ALTCS covers the long-term care services that Medicare doesn't provide, like ongoing personal care assistance and home-based support services.

programs.name: Arizona PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: All-in-one medical care and daily support for parents who need nursing home level care but want to stay home.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay at home, Arizona PACE provides all medical care, medications, therapies, meals, and personal care through one coordinated team. Because PACE covers everything under one program, your parent won't need separate doctors, specialists, or care coordinators — one interdisciplinary team manages it all.

Your parent must be 55+ and certified by Arizona's Medicaid agency (AHCCCS) as needing nursing home level of care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other essential activities). If they qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid, all services are free with no copays or deductibles. If they have Medicare only, private pay costs approximately $7,000+ per month.

PACE is only available in specific service areas across Arizona, not statewide. Most participants (90%+) are dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid, making this essentially a free comprehensive care program for qualifying seniors.
programs.savingsSource: Free service for Medicare/Medicaid dual eligibles

summary: Able to live safely at home with support
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by AHCCCS as needing nursing home level of care (requires daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Live in a PACE organization service area
Able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, hospice, or Medicare prescription drug plans
Must agree to receive all care through PACE team only

applicationGuide.summary: Call the AHCCCS helpline at (877) 382-2363 to start the nursing home certification process, then contact your local PACE organization.

steps.title: Check if PACE is available in your area
steps.description: Use the National PACE Association finder at npaonline.org to see if there's a PACE organization serving your parent's address. PACE is not available statewide in Arizona.

steps.title: Get nursing home level certification
steps.description: Call AHCCCS at (877) 382-2363 to begin the certification process. Your parent must be assessed and certified as needing nursing home level care, even if they plan to pay privately.

steps.title: Contact your local PACE organization
steps.description: Once you confirm PACE availability in your area, contact that specific PACE center directly. Each organization handles its own enrollment and has different wait times.

steps.title: Complete health assessment
steps.description: PACE staff will conduct a comprehensive assessment of your parent's medical, functional, and social needs to develop their care plan.

steps.title: Disenroll from conflicting programs
steps.description: Your parent must disenroll from Medicare Advantage, Medicare prescription drug plans, hospice, or other conflicting programs before PACE enrollment is complete.

applicationGuide.tip: Start the AHCCCS certification process early — this clinical assessment is required even for private-pay participants and can take time to complete.

documentsNeeded: Medicare card (Parts A and B)
Medicaid card if applicable
Social Security award letter
Birth certificate or passport for age verification
Proof of Arizona residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or state ID)
Complete list of current medications with dosages
Recent medical records and hospital discharge summaries
Contact information for all current doctors and specialists
Insurance cards for any supplemental coverage
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Income documentation (Social Security, pension, retirement statements)
Property documents if applying for ALTCS coverage
Legal documents for power of attorney or guardianship if applicable

contacts.label: AHCCCS Helpline
contacts.description: Arizona Medicaid agency for nursing home certification

contacts.label: National PACE Association
contacts.description: Find local PACE organizations at npaonline.org

applicationNotes: Even private-pay participants must complete the AHCCCS nursing home level certification — this clinical assessment is mandatory for all PACE enrollment
Your parent cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage and PACE simultaneously — they must choose one or the other
PACE is voluntary but comprehensive — once enrolled, your parent must receive all covered services through the PACE team only
Wait times and availability vary significantly by PACE organization and location within Arizona

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current doctors if they join PACE?
faqs.answer: No, PACE participants must receive all covered medical care through the PACE interdisciplinary team. Your parent will be assigned new doctors, specialists, and care coordinators who work together at the PACE center. This coordination is how PACE manages comprehensive care, but it means leaving current providers.

faqs.question: What if my parent has Medicare but doesn't qualify for Medicaid — can they still get PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes, but they'll pay private fees of approximately $7,000+ per month. They still need AHCCCS certification for nursing home level of care, but the financial eligibility doesn't matter for private-pay participants. Only about 10% of PACE participants pay privately.

faqs.question: How do I know if PACE is available where my parent lives?
faqs.answer: PACE is not available statewide in Arizona — only in specific service areas where PACE organizations operate. Use the finder at npaonline.org to search by your parent's zip code. If no PACE organization serves their area, they cannot access this program.

faqs.question: Can my parent leave PACE if they don't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes, PACE participation is voluntary and your parent can disenroll at any time. However, they'll need to find new doctors, re-enroll in Medicare Advantage or other coverage they left, and coordinate their own care again. There's no penalty for leaving.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent needs to move to a different area of Arizona?
faqs.answer: If they move outside their current PACE service area, they'll need to disenroll and find a new PACE organization in their new location — if one exists there. PACE enrollment is tied to specific geographic service areas, not the state overall.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover emergency care if my parent is traveling?
faqs.answer: Yes, emergency and urgent care is covered anywhere in the United States. However, routine care must still be provided through their home PACE center. For extended travel, they should coordinate with their PACE care team in advance.

faqs.question: Can I apply for PACE while my parent is still in the hospital?
faqs.answer: Yes, and this is often the best time to start the process. Hospital discharge planners can help initiate PACE enrollment, and the nursing home level certification may be easier to complete while your parent is receiving intensive medical care.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs more care than PACE can provide at home?
faqs.answer: PACE covers nursing home care when needed — participants don't lose coverage if they require facility-based care temporarily or permanently. The PACE team coordinates all levels of care from home services to nursing home placement as needs change.

programs.name: Arizona Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: If your parent is on Medicare with income under $1,796/month, Arizona can pay their Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays.

localEntities.name: AHCCCS central: 701 E. Jefferson St, Phoenix, AZ

programs.intro:
If your parent is on Medicare and has limited income, Arizona's Medicare Savings Programs can pay their Medicare costs. The programs cover Medicare Part B premiums (~$185/month in 2026), deductibles, and copays depending on income level. Your parent automatically gets Extra Help with prescription drug costs, capping generic medications at $12.65 each.

There are three programs based on income: QMB covers the most costs for those under $1,330/month income, SLMB covers Part B premiums for those under $1,596/month, and QI covers Part B premiums for those under $1,796/month (single person). Arizona has no asset limits, making these programs easier to qualify for than in many states. Because these programs coordinate with Medicare rather than replacing it, your parent keeps their Medicare benefits while getting help with the costs.
programs.savingsSource: Part B premium savings alone ($185/month × 12 = $2,220); QMB participants save additional amounts on deductibles and copays

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in or eligible for Medicare Part A
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Must apply for other available benefits (SSI, pensions)
Must have Medicare Part B for SLMB and QI programs

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through AHCCCS at healthearizonaplus.gov or call 1-855-432-7587; processing takes 45-90 days.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect Medicare card, Social Security award letter, and proof of Arizona residency before starting your application.

steps.title: Apply through AHCCCS
steps.description: Submit online at healthearizonaplus.gov, call 1-855-432-7587, or mail completed AHCCCS-8011 form to AHCCCS, P.O. Box 19021, Phoenix, AZ 85005.

steps.title: Choose your priority level
steps.description: QMB provides the most coverage but has the lowest income limit ($1,330/month single). SLMB and QI have higher income limits but cover only Part B premiums.

steps.title: Follow up on QI applications
steps.description: QI has federal funding limits and operates first-come-first-served. Apply early in the year and ask about waitlist status when applying.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program may have waitlist due to federal funding caps; first-come-first-served with priority for renewals
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for the highest tier you might qualify for—if your income is close to $1,330/month, start with QMB since it covers the most costs.

documentsNeeded: Medicare card showing Part A and Part B enrollment
Social Security award letter or current statement
Proof of Arizona residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or bank statement)
Valid government-issued photo ID (driver's license or state ID)
Birth certificate or passport for proof of age
Pension or retirement income statements for the last month
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Proof of citizenship or legal residency status
Documentation of any other benefits received (SSI, SNAP, etc.)
Proof of application for other benefits if required to apply

contacts.label: AHCCCS Customer Service
contacts.description: Medicare Savings Program applications and questions

contacts.label: Medicare General Information
contacts.description: General Medicare questions and coordination

contacts.label: Area Agencies on Aging
contacts.description: Local assistance with applications and Medicare counseling

applicationNotes: Arizona has no asset limits, but you must still apply for other benefits like SSI first if potentially eligible
QMB provides the strongest protection—providers cannot bill you for covered Medicare services beyond what Medicare allows
QI requires annual renewal and has federal funding caps, so apply early in the calendar year for best chances
All three programs automatically qualify you for Extra Help with prescription drug costs, but you need to coordinate with your Part D plan

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.covers: Part A premium (if any), Part B premium (~$185/month), all deductibles, coinsurance, and copays

contentSections.content: Arizona Advantage: No Asset Limits. Unlike many states, Arizona does not count your parent's savings, home value, or other assets when determining eligibility. Only income matters.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for all three programs at the same time?
faqs.answer: No, these are income tiers for the same program. AHCCCS will automatically place your parent in the highest tier they qualify for based on their income. If income is $1,330/month or less, they get QMB; if it's $1,331-$1,596, they get SLMB; if it's $1,597-$1,796, they get QI.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits include a $20 general disregard, so $20 is automatically subtracted from gross income. If they're still over, they won't qualify for that tier but may qualify for a higher income tier with fewer benefits. For example, if income is $1,650/month, they won't qualify for SLMB but may qualify for QI.

faqs.question: Will this affect my parent's regular Medicare benefits?
faqs.answer: No, these programs work with Medicare, not instead of it. Your parent keeps all their Medicare benefits and providers. The difference is that Arizona pays the costs (premiums, deductibles, copays) instead of your parent paying out of pocket.

faqs.question: How does the QI waitlist work and how long is it?
faqs.answer: QI has federal funding caps, so if more people apply than there's funding for, AHCCCS maintains a first-come-first-served waitlist. People already on QI get priority for renewal. Apply early in the calendar year for the best chance of getting in.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of elderly parents. You'll need their Medicare card, Social Security information, and proof of income. If you're not their legal representative, you may need their written permission or to become an authorized representative through AHCCCS.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this and regular Arizona Medicaid?
faqs.answer: These are Medicare Savings Programs that only help with Medicare costs. They don't provide the full range of Medicaid services. However, people with QMB may also qualify for full Medicaid benefits, which would cover services Medicare doesn't, like long-term care.

faqs.question: Do providers sometimes still try to bill QMB participants?
faqs.answer: Unfortunately yes, some providers incorrectly bill QMB participants for copays or coinsurance. QMB participants cannot be billed for any Medicare-covered services beyond what Medicare allows. If this happens, contact AHCCCS at (855) 432-7587 and file a complaint with Medicare at 1-800-MEDICARE.

faqs.question: How does the automatic Extra Help with prescriptions work?
faqs.answer: All three programs automatically enroll your parent in Medicare's Extra Help program, which caps prescription costs. Generics cost $12.65 each and brand names cost $53.90 each in 2026. You don't need to apply separately—Medicare will coordinate this automatically once AHCCCS approves the application.

programs.name: Nutrition Assistance (NA) / SNAP in Arizona
programs.tagline: Monthly food assistance of $24-$300+ to help your parent afford groceries on a fixed income.
programs.intro:
If your parent is struggling to afford groceries on a fixed income, Arizona's SNAP program (called Nutrition Assistance or NA) provides monthly benefits loaded onto an EBT card for food purchases. Arizona has expanded eligibility beyond federal requirements, making it easier for seniors to qualify — households with someone 60+ can bypass the gross income test entirely and qualify using only net income after deductions for medical expenses, housing costs, and taxes.

Benefits range from a minimum of $24/month to $300+ depending on household size and income. Because Arizona counts medical expenses as deductions, a senior with high healthcare costs may qualify even with income that seems too high at first glance. The program covers most food items at grocery stores but cannot be used for alcohol, tobacco, or hot prepared foods.
programs.savingsSource: Minimum $24/month to estimated maximum based on household size

summary: Households with member 60+ bypass gross income test
Arizona resident with valid immigration status
otherRequirements: Arizona resident
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant (refugees, trafficking victims, Cuban/Haitian immigrants, Iraqi/Afghan immigrants with special status)
Social Security number or proof of application
Work 80 hours/month if able-bodied (many exceptions apply)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at arizonaselfhelp.org or call 1-855-777-8590 — processing time not specified by state.

steps.title: Screen for eligibility
steps.description: Visit arizonaselfhelp.org to use Arizona's screening tool and see if your parent likely qualifies before starting the full application.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Complete application online at arizonaselfhelp.org, call Family Assistance Administration at 1-855-777-8590, or visit your local DES office in person.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect income statements, housing costs, utility bills, Social Security numbers, and proof of Arizona residency for all household members.

steps.title: Attend interview if required
steps.description: DES may schedule a phone or in-person interview to review your application and documents — they will contact you if needed.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time not specified by Arizona DES
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has high medical expenses, make sure to document them — these are deducted from income and could make them eligible even with higher gross income.

documentsNeeded: Social Security numbers and birth dates for all household members
Proof of household income from jobs, child support, and other sources
Social Security award letters or pension statements
Housing costs documentation (rent receipts, mortgage statements, property tax)
Utility bills (phone, electric, gas, water)
Child care or dependent care cost receipts
Proof of Arizona residency (utility bill, lease agreement, state ID)
Proof of citizenship or lawful immigration status
Bank statements for all accounts
Medical expense receipts and bills

contacts.label: Arizona SNAP Hotline
contacts.description: Arizona SNAP program questions and assistance

contacts.label: Family Assistance Administration
contacts.description: General application assistance and benefits questions

contacts.label: Arizona Food Bank Network
contacts.description: Emergency food assistance while application processes

applicationNotes: Households with someone 60+ get special treatment — they can qualify using only net income and asset tests, bypassing the $2,412 gross income limit entirely
Arizona has expanded eligibility beyond federal SNAP requirements, so income limits are higher than in many other states
Medical expenses are fully deductible from gross income — seniors with high healthcare costs should document everything
Work requirements have many exceptions including disability, pregnancy, caring for someone under 18, unemployment benefits, and living in high unemployment areas

faqs.question: Can my 65-year-old parent qualify if their Social Security is $2,000/month but they have high medical bills?
faqs.answer: Yes, likely. Because your parent is 60+, they bypass the gross income test entirely. Their $2,000 Social Security gets reduced by medical expenses, housing costs, and other deductions. If the net amount after deductions is under $1,304/month, they qualify regardless of the gross $2,000.

faqs.question: What if my parent owns their home worth $300,000 — does that disqualify them?
faqs.answer: No, the primary home doesn't count toward the $4,500 asset limit regardless of value. Only cash, bank accounts, and vehicles above certain values count as assets. Most seniors qualify on assets unless they have significant savings or investments.

faqs.question: Can I apply for SNAP on behalf of my elderly parent who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you'll need legal authority to act on their behalf — either power of attorney or legal guardianship. DES will require documentation of your authority before processing an application in someone else's name.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually receive in benefits?
faqs.answer: The minimum is $24/month for most households. The maximum depends on their net income after all deductions — the lower the net income, the higher the benefit. A senior with $800 net monthly income will receive significantly more than someone with $1,200 net income.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent gets denied — can they reapply?
faqs.answer: Yes, they can reapply immediately or request a hearing if they believe the decision was wrong. Many initial denials are due to missing documentation or not claiming all allowable deductions. Call 1-855-432-7587 to understand the specific reason for denial.

faqs.question: Can my parent get SNAP and still apply for other food programs?
faqs.answer: Yes, SNAP doesn't prevent participation in other programs like Commodity Supplemental Food Program (CSFP) or food banks. However, some programs have their own income limits that may be different from SNAP's requirements.

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Up to $640/year toward your parent's electric and gas bills, plus emergency help during utility shutoffs.
programs.intro:
If your parent struggles with electric or gas bills, LIHEAP can provide up to $640 per year toward heating and cooling costs. Because this program prioritizes households with adults 60+, disabled individuals, veterans, or children under 6, your elderly parent likely qualifies for priority processing ahead of general applicants.

LIHEAP operates on seasonal windows — heating assistance runs December through March, while cooling assistance runs April through October or November depending on your county. The program also offers crisis benefits up to $500 for emergency situations like utility shutoffs or equipment failures.

Because funding is limited and distributed first-come-first-served within priority groups, apply early when your parent's season opens. There are no asset limits, but gross monthly income must stay below $2,807 for a single person or $3,671 for a couple.
programs.savingsSource: Standard benefit up to $640 per year for heating or cooling (minimum $160). Crisis benefit up to $500 for energy emergencies.

summary: Priority for age 60+, disabled, veterans, or young children
Must be U.S. citizen or qualifying immigrant
otherRequirements: U.S. citizenship, permanent legal residence, or qualifying immigration status (refugee, TPS)
Cannot have utility bill credit exceeding $500
Must provide proof of residence in Arizona

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at des.az.gov/liheap or call (866) 494-1981 — processing time varies but funding runs out quickly each season.

steps.title: Check your season dates
steps.description: Heating assistance: December 1 – March 31. Cooling assistance: April 1 – October 31 (Maricopa, Pima, Yuma counties) or April 1 – November 30 (other counties). Apply early when your season opens.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need ID, proof of citizenship, income verification for last 30 days, current utility bills, and household member information. Crisis applications also need disconnection or delinquent notices.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Online: des.az.gov/liheap. Phone: (866) 494-1981 or (833) 453-2142. Mail: Complete form EAP-1002A and send to PO Box 19130, Phoenix, AZ 85009-9998. Fax: (602) 612-8282.

steps.title: Wait for processing
steps.description: Priority households (elderly, disabled, veterans, young children) get processed first. You'll receive notification of approval and benefit amount, which goes directly to your utility company.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — apply early as funding is limited and agencies may stop accepting applications when funds are exhausted
applicationGuide.waitlist: Funding is limited and distributed until exhausted — agencies may close applications mid-season if no funds remain
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has received a disconnection notice, mention this as a crisis case which may get expedited processing.

documentsNeeded: State driver's license or Arizona ID card
Proof of U.S. citizenship or immigration status (birth certificate, passport, or immigration documents)
Proof of Arizona residence (lease agreement, deed, or property tax bill)
Social Security numbers for all household members
Birth certificates or school records for all household members
Income verification for all adults 18+ from last 30 days (pay stubs, Social Security award letters, pension statements)
Current electric bill showing account number and balance
Current gas or propane bill showing account number and balance
Utility disconnection notice or delinquent bill (if applying for crisis assistance)

contacts.label: Arizona LIHEAP Hotline
contacts.description: Main application and information line

contacts.label: Alternative LIHEAP Line
contacts.description: Secondary application and assistance line

contacts.label: Arizona 2-1-1
contacts.description: 24/7 helpline for local Community Action Programs and other assistance

applicationNotes: Crisis cases with active disconnection notices may receive expedited processing — mention this when applying
If you previously applied to Tribal LIHEAP and were denied, bring documentation of that denial
Roommates who share utility bills count as one household for income calculations
Priority is given to vulnerable households, not first-come-first-served, so apply even if the season has started

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for both heating and cooling assistance in the same year?
faqs.answer: No, households can only receive one LIHEAP benefit per federal fiscal year (October 1 – September 30). Choose the season when your parent's bills are highest, typically cooling in Arizona. Crisis assistance is separate and can be received in addition to regular benefits.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is slightly over the limit one month but usually qualifies?
faqs.answer: LIHEAP uses gross monthly income from the last 30 days, so timing matters. If your parent had unusual income (like a one-time payment), wait until the following month to apply when income returns to normal levels below $2,807 for a single person.

faqs.question: How does the priority system work if my parent is over 60?
faqs.answer: Households with members 60+, disabled individuals, veterans, or children under 6 get processed first within each local Community Action Program. This doesn't guarantee benefits, but your parent moves ahead of general applicants in line for limited funding.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you'll need legal authority like power of attorney or guardianship documentation. The application must include proof of your authority to act on your parent's behalf, and benefits will still be issued in your parent's name to their utility company.

faqs.question: What happens if funding runs out before my parent's application is processed?
faqs.answer: The local Community Action Program will put your parent on a waiting list for the next program year or season. Some agencies stop accepting applications entirely when funding is exhausted, so apply as early as possible when the season opens.

faqs.question: My parent has a $600 credit on their electric bill from overpaying — can they still get LIHEAP?
faqs.answer: No, households cannot receive LIHEAP benefits if they have utility bill credits exceeding $500. Your parent would need to use that credit first and reapply when the balance drops below $500 credit or shows an amount due.

faqs.question: Can my parent get LIHEAP and Weatherization Assistance Program benefits at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate programs. LIHEAP helps with current utility bills while Weatherization makes long-term home improvements to reduce energy costs. Apply to both programs through the same local Community Action Program office.

faqs.question: What counts as a crisis situation for the emergency $500 benefit?
faqs.answer: Crisis assistance covers utility disconnection notices, equipment failures (broken furnace or AC), or dangerous situations affecting health and safety. You must provide documentation like disconnection notices or repair estimates when applying for crisis funds.

programs.name: Arizona Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.tagline: Free home energy improvements that can reduce your parent's monthly utility bills and make their home more comfortable year-round.

localEntities.name: Pima County Community and Workforce Development (outside Tucson, Sahuarita, Oro Valley, Marana)

programs.intro:
If your parent's household income is below $31,120 per year (for one person) and they're struggling with high energy bills, Arizona's Weatherization Assistance Program provides free home improvements to reduce utility costs. The program installs insulation, seals air leaks, replaces inefficient heating and cooling equipment, and makes other energy-saving upgrades at no cost to eligible homeowners and renters. SRP customers can receive up to $9,000 in improvements on top of federal funding.

Because Arizona uses multiple utility companies and regional agencies to run this program, you'll need to contact different offices depending on where your parent lives and which utility company serves them. Priority is given to households with elderly residents, people with disabilities, and families with children, making this particularly relevant for senior households on fixed incomes.

summary: Household income at or below 200% of federal poverty level
Must be customer of participating Arizona utility
Priority for elderly, disabled, and families with children
otherRequirements: Must be homeowner or renter
Must be customer of participating utility (SRP, TEP, UniSource, or other Arizona providers)
Home must pass assessment and inspection for eligible improvements

applicationGuide.summary: Contact the agency that serves your parent's utility area by phone or email — there's no single statewide application.

steps.title: Identify your parent's utility provider
steps.description: Check their electric bill to see if they have SRP, TEP (Tucson Electric Power), UniSource, or another Arizona utility company.

steps.title: Contact the right agency for your area
steps.description: Tucson/South Tucson (TEP customers): Call All Thrive 365 at 520-485-4985 or email tucsonwap@allthrive365.org. Pima County outside Tucson: Call 520-724-2461. SRP customers in Maricopa County: Call 480-808-0429. Phoenix residents: Email rehab.nsd@phoenix.gov or visit 200 W. Washington Street 4th Floor.

steps.title: Complete income verification
steps.description: Provide documentation showing your parent's household income is at or below the program limits for their household size.

steps.title: Schedule home assessment
steps.description: A certified energy auditor will visit your parent's home to identify which improvements will provide the most energy savings and determine eligibility for specific upgrades.

steps.title: Get improvements installed
steps.description: If approved, trained contractors will install approved weatherization measures at no cost to your parent.

applicationGuide.tip: Income limits vary slightly by utility provider, so if your parent is near the threshold, it's worth applying even if you're not sure they qualify.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID for all household members
Proof of homeownership (deed or mortgage statement) or rental agreement
Most recent electric and gas utility bills
Social Security award letters for all recipients
Proof of all household income from last 30 days (pay stubs, pension statements, benefits letters)
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Proof of residence (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Birth certificates or other proof of age for priority consideration

contacts.label: All Thrive 365 (Tucson/South Tucson)
contacts.description: TEP customers in Tucson and South Tucson area

contacts.label: Pima County Community Development
contacts.description: Pima County residents outside Tucson, Sahuarita, Oro Valley, Marana

contacts.label: SRP Weatherization Program
contacts.description: SRP customers in Maricopa County outside Phoenix/Mesa

contacts.label: City of Phoenix
contacts.description: Phoenix residents - email rehab.nsd@phoenix.gov or visit 200 W. Washington Street 4th Floor

applicationNotes: The agency you contact depends entirely on your parent's utility provider and location — there's no single statewide office
Income thresholds vary slightly between utility providers, so families near the limit should apply even if unsure about eligibility
Priority is given to elderly households, but this doesn't guarantee approval — funding remains limited
Home assessment is required before any work begins, and not all homes qualify for all types of improvements
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can renters apply or is this only for homeowners?
faqs.answer: Both homeowners and renters can apply. However, renters typically need written permission from their landlord before any weatherization work can begin on the property.

faqs.question: Which utility company does my parent have and which agency should I call?
faqs.answer: Check your parent's electric bill for the utility name. TEP (Tucson Electric Power) customers call All Thrive 365 at 520-485-4985. SRP customers call 480-808-0429. UniSource customers contact regional councils of government. Phoenix residents email rehab.nsd@phoenix.gov.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: Apply anyway, especially if they're close to the threshold. Income limits vary slightly by utility provider (SRP adds $10,760 per additional person while TEP/UniSource add $11,000), so they might qualify under one provider's rules even if not another's.

faqs.question: How long is the wait to get weatherization work done?
faqs.answer: Wait times aren't specified in available information and likely vary by region and funding availability. Contact your local agency directly for current processing times, as funding is limited statewide.

faqs.question: What types of improvements will they actually do to the house?
faqs.answer: Common improvements include insulation installation, caulking and weather-stripping, duct sealing, water heater insulation, air conditioning repairs or replacement, ENERGY STAR LED bulbs, and sun screens. The specific work depends on what the home assessment reveals will save the most energy.

faqs.question: Can I apply for both this program and LIHEAP energy assistance?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are complementary programs. LIHEAP provides bill payment assistance while weatherization makes long-term improvements to reduce future energy costs. Some families apply for both simultaneously.

faqs.question: Does the weatherization work come with any warranty or guarantee?
faqs.answer: The program uses certified contractors and follows federal weatherization standards, but specific warranty terms aren't detailed in available sources. Ask your local agency about warranties on equipment and workmanship when you apply.

faqs.question: What happens if the home assessment shows the house needs major repairs first?
faqs.answer: Not all homes qualify for all weatherization services. If the assessment reveals that basic structural or safety repairs are needed before weatherization work can begin, the program may not cover those preliminary repairs. Ask about this during the assessment process.

programs.name: Arizona State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.tagline: Free Medicare counseling to help your parent understand coverage options and save money on healthcare costs.

localEntities.name: Statewide helpline and azship.org; local examples: PCOA Tucson (520-790-7262), SEAGO Bisbee; full network via 1-800-432-4040

programs.intro:
If your parent has Medicare or is turning 65, Arizona SHIP provides free one-on-one counseling to help them understand their options and avoid costly mistakes. Trained counselors can help your parent compare Medicare Advantage plans, find prescription drug coverage, apply for programs that pay Medicare premiums (potentially saving up to $8,400/year), and protect against Medicare fraud.

Because SHIP counselors are unbiased and don't sell insurance, your parent gets honest advice about which plans work best for their specific health needs and budget. The service is available to all Medicare beneficiaries regardless of income, though priority goes to those with limited incomes or disabilities.

Arizona SHIP also includes the Senior Medicare Patrol program, which helps your parent spot and report Medicare fraud that could affect their benefits or lead to identity theft.

summary: Medicare beneficiary, family member, or caregiver
otherRequirements: Must be seeking help with Medicare-related issues

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide helpline at 1-800-432-4040 or submit an online form at azship.org — counselors return calls promptly to schedule appointments.

steps.title: Contact Arizona SHIP
steps.description: Call 1-800-432-4040 or fill out the intake form at azship.org. Phone lines are staffed during business hours.

steps.title: Schedule counseling session
steps.description: A trained counselor will call you back to schedule a phone, in-person, or virtual appointment based on your preference and location.

steps.title: Prepare for your session
steps.description: Gather your parent's Medicare card, current insurance information, and list of medications if you need help with prescription drug plans.

steps.title: Get personalized guidance
steps.description: Your counselor will review options, help with applications for money-saving programs, and provide written summaries of recommendations.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Counselors return calls or schedule appointments promptly after contact
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent received a Medicare fraud call or suspicious billing, ask specifically about Senior Medicare Patrol services during your appointment.

contacts.label: Arizona SHIP Statewide Helpline
contacts.description: Free Medicare counseling and fraud prevention assistance

contacts.label: PCOA Tucson Office
contacts.description: Local SHIP services for Pima County area

faqs.question: Can I get help for my parent even if they already have Medicare coverage?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors help people who are already enrolled in Medicare compare their current plans during Open Enrollment (October 15 - December 7), apply for programs that pay Medicare premiums and deductibles, and resolve billing or coverage problems. Many people save money by switching plans or adding benefits they didn't know were available.

faqs.question: Will the counselor try to sell my parent insurance?
faqs.answer: No. SHIP counselors are trained volunteers and staff who don't work for insurance companies and don't earn commissions. They provide unbiased information and won't pressure your parent to choose any particular plan. This is different from insurance agents who may only represent certain companies.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in a rural area of Arizona?
faqs.answer: Arizona SHIP serves all counties through local Area Agencies on Aging and regional providers. If there's no local office nearby, counseling is available by phone or virtual meeting. Call 1-800-432-4040 and they'll connect you with the counselor who serves your parent's area.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help if my parent has both Medicare and Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors specialize in helping people with both Medicare and Medicaid (called "dual eligibles") understand how their benefits work together and find plans that coordinate both programs. This is often confusing territory where expert guidance prevents costly mistakes.

programs.name: Congregate and Home-Delivered Meals
programs.tagline: Free or low-cost nutritious meals delivered to your parent's home or served at senior centers statewide.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggles to prepare meals or get to the grocery store, Arizona's meal programs can deliver hot, nutritious meals directly to their home up to 7 days per week. Because meals are delivered by trained staff, your parent also gets a daily wellness check — someone will notice if they don't answer the door or seem unwell.

The program offers two options: home-delivered meals (hot entrées plus cold items like salad and fruit) for homebound seniors, and congregate meals (hot lunches served at senior centers) for those who can travel and want social interaction. Priority goes to low-income seniors, minorities, and those in rural areas, though self-pay options around $9.49 per meal are available if your parent doesn't qualify for free service.

summary: Homebound or unable to prepare meals
Priority for low-income, rural, minority seniors
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound or unable to prepare meals independently due to illness, disability, or mobility issues. Congregate meals require ability to travel to senior center location.
otherRequirements: Referral often required from Area Agency on Aging, S.A.I.L., or ALTCS case managers
Cannot receive both congregate and home-delivered meals on same day (maximum 1 meal per day)
Self-pay options available if not eligible for free meals

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Area Agency on Aging helpline for screening — they determine eligibility and connect you to local providers.

steps.title: Call AAA helpline for initial screening
steps.description: Contact Area Agency on Aging at 602-264-4357 or 602-264-HELP. They will assess your parent's needs and determine eligibility for free meals versus self-pay options.

steps.title: Get referred to local provider
steps.description: Based on your location and needs, AAA will connect you to providers like YWCA Metropolitan Phoenix (623-931-7436) for Glendale area or Fountain Hills Community Center (480-816-5229, Mon-Thu 9am-4pm).

steps.title: Complete provider intake
steps.description: The local provider will schedule meal delivery or congregate site visits. Prepare documentation of age, disability status, or homebound condition as requested.

steps.title: Begin meal service
steps.description: Home-delivered meals typically include hot entrées and cold items. Delivery schedules vary by provider — some offer weekday hot meals plus weekend frozen options.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has Medicaid/ALTCS, contact their case manager first — they may have faster access to covered meal services through Mom's Meals or other health plan providers.

documentsNeeded: Proof of age (driver's license, birth certificate, or state ID)
Documentation of disability if applying under age 60
Medical documentation of homebound status or inability to prepare meals
Proof of residence in Arizona
Referral from AAA, S.A.I.L., or ALTCS case manager if applicable
Income documentation for financial assistance screening (though no fixed limits apply)
Special dietary needs documentation from healthcare provider if required

contacts.label: Area Agency on Aging Helpline
contacts.description: Primary contact for eligibility screening and provider referrals

contacts.label: Area Agency on Aging (Alternative)
contacts.description: Same helpline with easy-to-remember number

contacts.label: YWCA Metropolitan Phoenix
contacts.description: Home-delivered and congregate meals in Glendale area

contacts.label: Fountain Hills Community Center
contacts.description: Local meal delivery service

applicationNotes: You must contact the AAA helpline first for screening — you cannot apply directly with local providers without referral
Medicaid/ALTCS members should check with their health plan case manager for covered meal services before applying through AAA
Self-pay options around $9.49 per meal are available if your parent doesn't qualify for free service but meets other requirements like being 80+ or post-hospital
Delivery days and meal types vary by provider — some Phoenix areas offer weekday hot meals plus weekend frozen meals

faqs.question: Can my parent get both home-delivered meals and eat at the senior center?
faqs.answer: No, your parent cannot receive both congregate and home-delivered meals on the same day. The program allows a maximum of one meal per day. They'll need to choose between home delivery or senior center dining based on their mobility and social needs.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for free meals but they still need help?
faqs.answer: Self-pay options are available for around $9.49 per meal. Some providers like Fountain Hills Community Center offer self-pay if your parent meets other criteria like being 80+, post-hospital recovery, or having a caregiver situation.

faqs.question: How quickly can meal delivery start once approved?
faqs.answer: Processing times aren't specified, but scheduling varies by provider. Popular programs may have wait times, so call the AAA helpline at 602-264-4357 as soon as possible to get the screening process started.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my parent who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family caregivers can call the Area Agency on Aging helpline to start the application process. If your parent has ALTCS or other case management services, their case manager can also initiate meal services.

faqs.question: What if my parent has special dietary needs like diabetes or kidney disease?
faqs.answer: The program can accommodate special diets for medical needs with proper documentation from a healthcare provider. Mention dietary restrictions when calling the AAA helpline at 602-264-4357 for initial screening.

faqs.question: Does the meal program cover weekends?
faqs.answer: Coverage varies by provider and location. Some Phoenix area providers offer up to 7 meals per week, including weekday hot meals and weekend frozen options. The AAA helpline can tell you what's available in your parent's specific area.

faqs.question: What happens during the wellness check with meal delivery?
faqs.answer: Delivery staff are trained to notice if your parent doesn't answer the door or seems unwell. This provides daily safety monitoring, but specific emergency protocols vary by provider. Ask about their wellness check procedures when setting up service.

faqs.question: Can my parent switch from congregate meals to home delivery if their mobility changes?
faqs.answer: Yes, needs can be reassessed if your parent's condition changes. Contact their current provider or the AAA helpline at 602-264-4357 to discuss switching from senior center meals to home delivery if they become less mobile.

programs.name: Arizona Long Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocate when your parent faces problems in their nursing home or assisted living facility.

localEntities.name: Maricopa County Area Agency on Aging
NACOG (serving Apache, Coconino, Navajo, and Yavapai counties)
Navajo Nation Area Agency on Aging

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in a nursing home, assisted living facility, or adult day care in Arizona and faces abuse, neglect, poor care, or wrongful eviction, a Long Term Care Ombudsman can investigate and advocate for them at no cost. These trained advocates have legal authority to enter facilities, review records, and mediate disputes between residents and staff.

The program serves any resident in Arizona long-term care facilities including nursing homes, assisted living, hospice centers, adult day care, and adult foster care homes. Because ombudsmen must have your parent's consent before investigating complaints, the resident (not just family) must authorize action. Ombudsmen investigate complaints about medication errors, inadequate food quality, facility cleanliness, billing disputes, and violations of resident rights.

summary: Resident of Arizona long-term care facility
Includes nursing homes and assisted living
otherRequirements: Must be a resident of a long-term care facility in Arizona (nursing homes, hospice centers, adult day care, assisted living facilities, or adult foster care homes)
Resident must consent to ombudsman investigation or advocacy

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional Area Agency on Aging or the state ombudsman office directly — no application forms required.

steps.title: Call your regional office
steps.description: Contact your county's Area Agency on Aging: Maricopa County (602) 264-4357, Pima County (520) 790-7262, Northern Arizona counties (928) 213-5215, or Yavapai County (928) 649-3763.

steps.title: Describe the situation
steps.description: Explain the problem your parent is experiencing in their facility. The ombudsman will determine if they can investigate based on your parent's consent.

steps.title: Get resident consent
steps.description: Your parent must agree to the ombudsman's involvement before any investigation can begin. The ombudsman will explain their role and get verbal or written consent.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is reluctant to speak up about facility problems, explain that ombudsmen are independent advocates, not facility staff, and their services are confidential.

contacts.label: Maricopa County Area Agency on Aging
contacts.description: Ombudsman services for Phoenix metro area

contacts.label: Pima County (Pima Council on Aging)
contacts.description: Ombudsman services for Tucson area

contacts.label: Northern Arizona NACOG
contacts.description: Apache, Coconino, and Navajo counties

contacts.label: State Ombudsman Program
contacts.description: Main state office for escalation or general questions

contacts.label: Adult Protective Services
contacts.description: Report serious abuse or neglect of vulnerable adults

faqs.question: Can I call the ombudsman if my parent is afraid to complain about their facility?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman can meet with your parent privately to explain their rights and the complaint process, but they cannot investigate without your parent's consent. Many residents feel more comfortable speaking up once they understand that ombudsmen are independent advocates, not facility employees, and that retaliation against residents for filing complaints is illegal.

faqs.question: What if the regional ombudsman doesn't resolve our problem?
faqs.answer: You can escalate to the DES oversight office at (602) 542-6446. For serious abuse or neglect, also contact Adult Protective Services at (877) 767-2385. If the issue involves Medicaid billing or services, contact the Arizona Health Care Cost Containment System (AHCCCS).

faqs.question: Can the ombudsman help if the facility wants to evict my parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, ombudsmen investigate inappropriate evictions and can advocate for proper notice and due process. Arizona law requires 30-day written notice for most evictions from assisted living facilities, and nursing homes must follow federal discharge planning requirements. The ombudsman can ensure the facility follows proper procedures.

stateOverview.intro: Arizona offers 8 financial benefit programs and 1 free resource to help your aging parent with healthcare costs, home care, food, utilities, and Medicare navigation. Benefits range from $24-$300/month for groceries through SNAP to full coverage of nursing home costs through ALTCS, Arizona's Medicaid program for seniors. Most programs have income limits around $1,500-$2,200/month for a single person, but some free resources like Medicare counseling are available regardless of income. Start by checking if your parent qualifies for Arizona's Medicare Savings Programs — these can immediately reduce monthly healthcare costs while you explore longer-term care options.

startHere.name: Arizona Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
startHere.why: Can immediately eliminate Medicare premiums and copays if your parent's income is under $1,796/month.

startHere.name: AHCCCS (Arizona Long Term Care System - ALTCS)
startHere.why: Arizona's Medicaid covers the biggest expenses — nursing home care, assisted living, and home care services.

startHere.name: Nutrition Assistance (NA) / SNAP in Arizona
startHere.why: Monthly grocery assistance starts quickly and provides $24-$300+ per month in immediate relief.

startHere.name: Arizona State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
startHere.why: Free Medicare counseling helps you understand all healthcare options before making expensive decisions.

byNeed.need: Help paying for home care or nursing home
programs: AHCCCS (Arizona Long Term Care System - ALTCS)
ALTCS Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waiver
byNeed.description: Arizona's Medicaid programs can cover in-home care, assisted living, or nursing facility costs for qualifying seniors.

byNeed.need: Medicare premium and medical cost relief
programs: Arizona Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
byNeed.description: Arizona pays Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays for seniors with income under $1,796/month.
Monthly grocery assistance plus free meal delivery or senior center dining options.

programs: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
byNeed.description: Annual assistance with electric and gas bills plus free home improvements to reduce energy costs.

programs: Arizona State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
byNeed.description: Free Medicare counseling available to all Arizona seniors regardless of income.

quickFacts: Arizona uses AHCCCS (Arizona Health Care Cost Containment System) instead of traditional Medicaid for senior long-term care coverage
Most Arizona benefit programs have income limits between $1,500-$2,200/month for a single person
Arizona PACE programs provide all-in-one medical care and daily support as an alternative to nursing home placement
SHIP counseling is free for all Arizona Medicare beneficiaries at 1-800-432-4040
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Most Arizona programs are benefits that require meeting income and asset limits — like ALTCS (Arizona's Medicaid) which covers nursing home costs but requires income under specific thresholds. However, Arizona's State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) provides free Medicare counseling to any Arizona senior regardless of income, helping them understand coverage options and find savings on healthcare costs.
